content:
Why Is It So Hard for Law Students to Find an Internship Today?

Every law student has heard this advice:

"Do internships. Gain practical exposure. Build your CV."

It sounds simple—until you actually start looking.

Behind every successful internship offer are hundreds of unanswered emails, rejected applications, and students wondering what more they could have done.

The reality is that today's students are trying.

They spend hours researching law firms, customizing resumes, writing cover letters, networking on LinkedIn, and sending application after application. Yet, many never receive a response.

So, what is stopping them?

In my opinion, it isn't a lack of hard work.

It's the system.

The legal industry often expects freshers to have experience before giving them an opportunity to gain experience. We ask students to prove themselves without first giving them a platform to learn.

Many students are willing to start with small law firms. They're not chasing prestigious names—they're looking for a chance to understand the profession, contribute, and grow.

But even then, the journey forward isn't easy.

Instead of spending their early years learning the practical side of law, students spend months simply trying to understand the recruitment process—who to contact, what recruiters expect, how many follow-ups are appropriate, and why applications go unanswered.

By the time they reach their final year, many are mentally exhausted.

Some lose confidence.

Some begin questioning their abilities.

Some even start exploring careers outside the legal profession—not because they don't love law, but because they couldn't find a doorway into it.

The legal profession cannot expect experienced professionals if it doesn't invest in inexperienced students.

Every senior advocate, partner, or legal professional was once a fresher hoping that someone would believe in their potential rather than their experience.

Perhaps the real question isn't:

"Why don't students have enough experience?"

The real question is:

"Why has getting that first opportunity become so difficult?"

It's time we build a culture that values potential as much as experience. Because the future of the legal profession depends not only on great lawyers—but also on giving aspiring lawyers their very first chance.

I'd love to hear your thoughts.

If you're a law student, what's been your biggest challenge while applying for internships? And if you're a recruiter or lawyer, what changes do you think the system needs?
comment: Beyond demand, many small firms lack structured internship intake and time to filter cold applications, so referrals and college pipelines dominate decisions
